channel.title: Comments for page "The High Court With The Magic Army"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "The High Court With The Magic Army"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is this thing i am wondering. If the story of "i quit" comes before this, then who is the new O5-12? In the latest story added Troy Lament has became O5-12, and over here he represents 12, is that some sort of connection?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love the characterization of the O5 as actual people, and not some eldritch horror than many people fear them to be. It was interesting to see how they all have different personalities and views. Feels comforting to know that the O5 are prone to the same insecurities as the average Joe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These are excellent questions, which are fun to answer!

To your overall point: The Foundation hides that the O5s have anomalous traits, some more than others. The vast majority of the Foundation has no idea, including with Four, who only flaunts it within the O5 circle. But…

+ show block
– hide block

Why would the foundation be afraid of 9 when 4 is openly anomalous and flaunts it? Shouldn’t his perspective be a tad more dangerous? Not that I don’t like 4, it’s just jarring.

The Foundation isn't afraid of Nine. The O5 Council is afraid of Nine.

You're also completely on the right track reading this in combination with what Moose (the character) says in the Magic Orientation. Ten herself isn't telling Nine the whole story — because while she really likes Nine, she's afraid of Nine too.

As for Four… look at his perspective, and the perspective other O5s have of him. The very literal perspective is that he's the most exaggeratedly anomalous O5. He seems like an outlier. Unique, even. This may not even be true. But the 'perspective' is certainly deliberate — the perspective of "Only Four is like this" and "Only Four can be like this." And even then, no one outside a very select group of people (mostly the higher echelons of the Foundation) know about his anomalous traits.

Plus… he mostly uses those traits for his personal actions, which are done for outside-Foundation purposes (diplomacy). Notice how even some or all other O5s don't really get how he works and don't try to duplicate it. Salt, our "closest person in the world to an O5" narrator, knows literally nothing at all about it except what she's seen or heard of him do.

See, the whole thing about the O5s is that people see them as unique. And that part is true, relatively speaking. The idea of "everyone can use some form of real-life magic" is vastly, infinitely more dangerous, especially because it's technically true even now.

No one thinks they can become an O5, and even if they do, they think they're taking one of 13 unique slots (up to a couple dozen max if you believe there are multiple O5s). That isn't changing the world. It's just another "anomaly". "Only the O5 Council can be like this — no one else can be like this."

Same reason there can be thousands or millions of Foundation personnel without their perspective completely upending mainline reality — they think of these anomalies as anomalies, aka, not normal.

Also, 10’s dream talking. It felt…like a bit of a stretch? An unlikely coincidence that she happened to be dreaming about that?

It's not a coincidence. She's completely obsessed with the topics she's dreaming about and everything we see her do in this story is specifically because of the things that are causing her to have semi-prophetic dream/hallucinations.

Long post collapsed. ~Zyn

(There's more to it that I didn't want to go into because of space constraints, but she also deliberately triggered the dreams. Not those specific dreams, but the state that caused the dreams. Among other things, she's trying to accelerate the natural process of "figuring things out subconsciously" that everyone has when they "sleep on it". It's doing a number on her and is a really bad idea, but it's also kind of working. At least here. She was afraid of hurting herself and [other things], which is why she has Salt watch her.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although we’re never given any exact information about the O5s, it’s fun to wonder, right?
I will say that I like the more human aspects felt a lot more interesting than the anomalous nature of certain members. You’re really good at writing people with different views and personalities, and I feel like you don’t need to also give these people magical mystery. I just got off at your magic orientation, where you also emphasize the importance of perspective as the reason for the relatively low usage of magic in the foundation, and it feels weird that the people heading it should have that aspect, aside from 006 (and the oracles, I guess.) Why would the foundation be afraid of 9 when 4 is openly anomalous and flaunts it? Shouldn’t his perspective be a tad more dangerous? Not that I don’t like 4, it’s just jarring.
Also, 10’s dream talking. It felt…like a bit of a stretch? An unlikely coincidence that she happened to be dreaming about that?
But overall, I liked it. I liked the characters, they all felt possible.
+1.

item.title: Re: I feel stupid
item.content:encoded:
First of all: does this make The Flytrap canon to Resurrection?

[a year later…]

I wanted to leave its canonicity to Resurrection ambiguous.

The Flytrap informs this tale down to its bones, though. I would say all of the content of the Flytrap is certainly canon to Resurrection, whether or not the actual events are.

item.title: Re: I feel stupid
item.content:encoded: Ah, thank you!

item.title: Re: I feel stupid
item.content:encoded: 963-2 comes from two of TheDuckman's tales. He was the last O5-9, who tried to become immortal and ended up as a save scummer.

item.title: I feel stupid
item.content:encoded: First of all: does this make The Flytrap canon to Resurrection? Or are you just linking it for the character concepts? Second, I don't think I quite understand who 963-2 is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
edit: was wrong about how long her speech was, but I still think it's bland and unconvincing. It doesn't address much of the concerns the naysayers made, from my reading.

Correct, it doesn't. One and Twelve even point that out.

Part of this was that I didn't want to make it longer. This won't necessarily help this work for you, but I'll write it out what politically happened anyway in case you're curious:

+ show block
– hide block

One wanted to abstain in the first place. But Ten didn't sufficiently defend Alpha-9 to his liking, and she didn't say anything. One couldn't see the point of standing by and letting the experiment unfold when Ten, its biggest backer, seemed to have given up championing it.

When she did break silence, and made an argument, One decided to abstain. It helped that it would've been politically better for her to apologize and back off to avoid censure, but she didn't. (She took a leap of faith.)

Six was already ambivalent, and felt similarly to One, and also felt One's opinion was worth taking into account, so he changed to abstain as well (One's vote had swayed him to the Nays in the first place).

And Seven is… Seven. She was already helping Alpha-9, despite her initial Nay vote. So she was willing to change her vote to Support once One and Six had sent a message that it was tactically viable to do so.

At that point, Alpha-9 passed. The rest was just Eight fucking up. And it STILL doesn't pass with a majority 7 votes.

– hide block

I've added a couple cut lines back in to indicate that Ten didn't say very much until her mini-speech, because I see that they're necessary. (That doesn't necessarily fix it for everyone, mind you.)

Feedback appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This… Was pretty disappointing.

I liked the characterizations of the O5 for the most part, but I got the sense that the whole point of this article was mounting tension going up to the vote.

Then the vote comes, and doesn't pass… Then 10 stands up for what I'm assuming was to be a passionate speech that turns the tide! Instead she rattles off a (frankly) unconvincing speech, and the powerful, wise council who I would have hoped put SOME thought behind their original votes basically says "eh, ok". All this build up for that?

edit: was wrong about how long her speech was, but I still think it's bland and unconvincing. It doesn't address much of the concerns the naysayers made, from my reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've seen some crazy lineups for the Council, but this one's a real standout. In particular, I like Eight, which is a shame, since by coming first, he overshadows Nine, who I also liked quite a lot. Thirteen was kind of ridiculous, but then Scantron reminded me where the name comes from, so I'm rather agog. The final scene is great, tense diplomacy is always exciting when written well.

Imma fix a couple things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is exactly how I would view the O5 council voting, even if the characters do not match my headcanon. Great tale overall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel that this O5 concept is interesting. Well done. This is why I like resurrection: a series of tales that'll always keep you wanting more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Preface: This strongly clashes with my O5 head-canon.

Main body: I strongly like this.

Conclusions: +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
yay I got thanked

also yay O5-related stuff

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep, they are directly from the RP's notes. I liked the detail, so I stole it, but I didn't realize that you'd created them.

I'm sorry the tale didn't work for you, though.

Regarding the ending:

show commentary
– hide block

A lot of the way it's constructed relates to Council dynamics that I tried to imply more than directly state. I didn't want to make everything too on the nose in the story. And also, length was a concern. But if it didn't come across for you, it didn't come across.

I hope it works for others.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… I may very well be imagining this, but I distinctly remember inventing the buttersquirrels when I GMed in the Tamlin House RP. They eat shoes.

But this tale doesn't focus on what I really think are the cool parts of Resurrection,and maybe I'm taking the wrong thing away from this, but I would have preferred to read something overall less melodramatic.

And the ending kind of felt like an ass pull.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really, just awesome. Lots of cool little mini-stories, all tied into a long, suspenseful arc, and with how things have gone with responses to the Resurrection series, I felt that the ending could have truly gone either way.

Well written, to say the least. A good ending to this section of the series, leaving the audience ready and eager for the next season to start up.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a nest of squirrels with butterfly wings

/INCOHERENT SCREECH

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, I am seriously indebted to Light for The Flytrap and Bright for Ecce Perago and Annon, without which this tale would not exist.

Thank you also to:

  • Clef for an absolutely incredible amount of editing and improvements — just, holy shit. Clef did an unbelievable amount of work on this.
  • TroyL for a ton of concepts for the O5 Council, many of which I used here: in particular the concepts/characters for One & Three [partially], Eight, Ten, Eleven, Twelve, Lament, and the final scene.
  • Light for practically writing Five's scene, concepts/characters for Five and Seven, and helping inspire the entire story.
  • DrMann for practically writing the first half of Four's scene, also Four's concept. Also a lot of improvement on One's scene, and much of One's concept/character.
  • Bright for the concepts/characters for One (partially), Three (partially), Six, the original Nine, and Thirteen. Plus inspiring Ten, Eleven, and Twelve, and helping inspire the entire story.
  • Photosynthetic for Nine's concept/character and scene.
  • Kestin for a lot of very helpful critique.
  • …and a ton of other people for a lot of very helpful critique.
  • …and a ton of other people whose concepts I ripped off homaged/referenced/used in this.

Fun fact: each scene in this tale was originally two to five times longer, with most of the same content. Bright pointed out it was bloated, and Clef did the lion's share of the editing. <3

.
Hide Spoiler

Everything Ten says refers to an already written or not-yet-posted story on the site.

Hide Spoiler
